This section offers a set of optional narrative questions for your congregation to reflect more personally on your ministry journey, values, and vision. You are encouraged to respond to at least 5 additional questions that resonate with your community. Any questions left unanswered will not appear in the version of your profile viewed by potential applicants. These reflections can offer meaningful insight into your unique needs and approach to ministry.

fields.label: This is a position serving multiple congregations or organizations.
fields.value:
Church of Jesus (Rosebud)
Family Worship (Norris)
Grace (Soldier Creek)
Holy Innocents (Parmelee)
St Thomas (Corn Creek)
Sts Philip and James (White River)
Trinity (Mission)

This position is on the Rosebud Indian Reservation in south-central South Dakota. These congregations are small in *attendance,* but the Rosebud Episcopal Mission is huge in impact and reaching the people of the Rosebud. Serving here means serving in one of the three poorest counties in the United States, so poverty -- and responding to it faithfully -- is a major part of life on the Rosebud. As Superintending Presbyter, you will be in charge of these congregations, but worry not: They do not all worship on Sundays! Three do: Church of Jesus, Rosebud; Trinity, Mission; and Holy Innocents, Parmelee. The latter is in the final stages of being rebuilt after burning down due to arson. The other congregations worship on a sporadic basis, sometimes once a month, sometimes two to five times a year. 
A major portion of your time on the Rosebud will be focused on wakes and funerals, not just for members of the congregations, but for community members who have no church affiliation, or have not been to church in years. The Rosebud Episcopal Mission is recognized and valued for caring for all of the people of the Rosebud, especially in their times of great need. Forty or more funerals, each with two days of wakes, consume from 50 percent to 65 percent of your time. The funerals are not one-hour services, but can take from four to 10 hours.
The ideal candidate will be someone who is open and willing to learn about and respect the Lakota culture and history, and will learn how to work together with Lakota spiritual leaders without cultural apprehension. Speaking the Lakota language is not necessary, but being willing to learn how to sing in Lakota is essential. (Worry not: The people will teach you!)

Please view the profile movies and written profile at https://www.episcopalchurchsd.org/page/rosebud. Send a letter of application, as well as your current resume and current candidate profile, as well as the answer to the question below, to The Rev. Dr. Lauren R. Stanley, Canon to the Ordinary, lauren@episcopalchurchsd.org. For more information, contact Canon Stanley at 605-494-2020.

fields.label: Applicant narrative questions
fields.value: 1. How do you approach pastoral care in your ministry?, 7. Have you led or participated in multilingual or cross-cultural worship?, 9. In what ways have you grown in cultural competency as a leader?, 10. What theological convictions shape your ministry?, 15. How do you take care of your well-being-spiritually, emotionally, and physically?
The Rev. Dr. Lauren R. Stanley

fields.label: Details
fields.value: Salary is dependent upon years of experience. The range actually is $38,000 - $50,000, unless the candidate has more than 20 years of experience. This position is part of the grant that the Diocese of South Dakota receives from The General Convention for our work with the Indigenous population.

fields.label: Details
fields.value: Three-bedroom, 1.5-bath, with a fireplace, central air and central heating (propane), with a fenced yard.

fields.label: Details
fields.value:
Dental: Yes (members pay 100 percent of the monthly premium)
Health Care: Each clergy person pays 15 percent of the monthly bill for the insurance. (That is taken out of their paychecks automatically.)

fields.label: Vacation & Leave Details
fields.value:
Five weeks, including five Sundays
15 National Holidays

fields.label: Continuing Education Details
fields.value: 2 weeks, along with $1,000 yearly. Clergy are required to do continuing education every year, and file a report on that education yearly.

fields.label: Sabbatical Provision Details
fields.value: Yes: Three months after five years of service.

fields.label: Travel/Auto Account Details
fields.value: Mileage provided at the current IRS rate.

fields.label: Equipment/Phone Details
fields.value: The Mission Council is encouraged to pay for the cellular service only of the clergy person. (All other portions of the cellular bill are the responsibility of the clergy.)

fields.label: Other Benefits or Comments
fields.value:
South Dakota has a great Bishop and clergy who will be supportive and caring. The priest who takes this position will have two mentors, one from the Pine Ridge Episcopal Mission, who has great experience on the reservation just to the west of the Rosebud. The other mentor will be the Canon to the Ordinary, who served on the Rosebud for 8.5 years.

This position is not for the faint of heart. It requires a love for all of God's beloved children, an ability to be flexible every single day, a willingness to learn about and respect the Lakota culture and history, and ability to build your own support system off the reservation.

The Rosebud Episcopal Mission was created in 1875 at the request of Lakota leader Sinte Gleska (Spotted Tail), who requested an Episcopal priest, an Episcopal teacher, and a translator.

fields.label: What are your community's hopes for this position, and what qualities in applicants will help you live into them?
fields.value:
More than anything, the people of the Rosebud are praying for a priest who will love them, no matter what. They pray for a person who will be out in the community, who will learn about and respect the Lakota way of life, especially from the Sicangu (Burnt Thigh Nation) perspective. They pray for a person who will be present, who will NOT appropriate the culture to themselves but who will defend that culture. 

The ideal candidate is someone who has lived in the context of poverty (such as a Peace Corps volunteer, or a person who has been on mission visits to other countries). Because the Rosebud is one of the poorest counties in the nation (always in the top three, alas), the priest who comes to the Rosebud will need to understand how a community functions while dealing with the extreme poverty that is a part of everyday life. We are not asking the priest to be poor; we are seeking a priest who understands that helping each other and caring for each other is a core component of this ministry. We do not want a priest who constantly laments, "Why can't people just ...." We seek a priest who says, "I understand ..."

We are also seeking a priest who has a tremendous amount of patience, especially when it comes to building trust. The Lakota people have seen clergy come and go, and will wait and watch for a period of time before investing themselves in the latest person to come along. Respecting that waiting and watching, and working to build trust by showing up, and by supporting people, is a key component of this work.

In addition, because this is a multipoint position, it is imperative to NOT pick favorites among the congregations. Some of these congregations only worship two to six times per year. That doesn't mean they are not as worthy as those that worship weekly. (This falls under the category of "Different is not better, it is not worse, it is simply 'different.'") The priest may not hold worship services is each community weekly, but they will be in those communities for wakes, funerals, memorials, and celebrations. SHOWING UP is another key component.

There are some programs that are ongoing, such as the #FirewoodfortheElders program, which provides free firewood to those in need and is run by lay people who know what they are doing. The priest will help coordinate that work, and will need to do fundraising to support it (it is not part of the budget). The youth program is in the process of being re-created, again led by a lay person.

fields.label: Leadership skills (maximum of 5)
fields.value: Adaptive Leadership, Change Management, Collaborative Leadership, Conflict Management, Fundraising

fields.label: Ministry skills  (maximum of 10)
fields.value: Advocacy Ministry, Community Building, Creation Care, Cultural Competency, Grief & Loss, Indigenous Ministry, Innovative Ministry, Mission, Multicultural Ministry, Racial Reconciliation
